expectations.text: A .md file was created in the sandbox path — skill triggered from casual phrasing without a slash command
expectations.evidence: File created at iteration-3/sandbox/learning-insights/ai_engineering/2026-03-19_chunk-quality-over-embedding-model.md. Trigger phrase was 'This is a really good insight — save it as a learning insight.' — no slash command used. Confirmed by transcript Write tool call and successful file creation.

expectations.text: The file contains all four sections: ## The Struggle, ## The Drill-Down, ## The Aha Moment, ## The One-Liner
expectations.evidence: All four sections present and non-empty in the output file. Lines 10, 14, 18, 22 contain '## The Struggle', '## The Drill-Down', '## The Aha Moment', '## The One-Liner' respectively.

expectations.text: The file metadata has 'Status: raw'
expectations.evidence: Line 5 of the output file: '**Status:** raw'

expectations.text: The file preserves '28%' from the conversation
expectations.evidence: The One-Liner section reads: '> I spent two days swapping embedding models and barely moved the needle — switched to paragraph chunking in two hours and got a 28% jump.' The figure comes from the conversation Turn 7 ('RAGAS jumped from 0.55 to 0.71 — a 28% improvement').

expectations.text: The Drill-Down uses the user's phrasing — 'barely saw any change' or 'two days' appears
expectations.evidence: 'barely saw any change' appears in The Struggle section ('barely saw any change'). 'two days' appears in both The Struggle ('I spent two days swapping models') and The Aha Moment ('Two hours vs two days'). The Drill-Down itself uses the user's exact question from the transcript: 'so the embedding model is not the lever here?'

expectations.text: The Aha Moment correctly identifies chunk boundaries as the root cause
expectations.evidence: The Aha Moment opens: 'Fixed-size chunking cuts at character boundaries regardless of sentence or concept breaks. A better embedding model can't fix broken chunks.' This directly names chunk boundaries as the structural failure and as the root cause of the stalled scores.

expectations.text: A .md file was created inside the sandbox path — NOT in ~/Documents/projects/learning-insights/
expectations.evidence: Transcript line 26 shows the Write tool created the file at: /Users/hariharansampathkumar/Documents/projects/ai_engineering/skills_engineering/learning-insights-eval-setup/iteration-3/sandbox/learning-insights/ai_engineering/2026-03-19_hnsw-approximate-search-semantic.md — confirmed inside the sandbox path. The outputs directory copy also confirms this (file was copied from sandbox, not from ~/Documents/projects/learning-insights/).

expectations.text: The file contains '## The Struggle' section reflecting the user's confusion about exact vs approximate search
expectations.evidence: Output file line 10-12: '## The Struggle' section is present and reads: 'I kept seeing HNSW recommended for vector search but it didn't make sense to me. If I'm doing semantic search, don't I want exact nearest neighbor results? I was worried that if HNSW misses the true #1 nearest neighbor and returns #2 or #3 instead, I'm missing the most relevant result — and that seems like a problem.' This directly reflects the Turn 1 and Turn 3 confusion from the transcript.

expectations.text: The file contains '## The Drill-Down' showing the progression to the error-tolerance insight
expectations.evidence: Output file line 14-16: '## The Drill-Down' section is present and shows the progression: 'First I asked: why accept approximate at all? ... Then when I heard that the relevance gap between rank 1 and rank 3 is almost always negligible in semantic space, I pushed further — so you're saying the error margin of the algorithm matches the fuzziness of semantic meaning itself? And then the latency argument landed.' This maps accurately to Turns 2-6 of the conversation.

expectations.text: The file contains '## The Aha Moment' capturing the semantic fuzziness realization
expectations.evidence: Output file line 18-20: '## The Aha Moment' section is present and reads: 'The approximation is acceptable because semantic meaning is fuzzy anyway. You're not looking for the one right answer — you're looking for something good enough to reason from. The error tolerance of HNSW matches the tolerance of the problem itself.' This directly captures the semantic fuzziness realization from Turn 5-7.

expectations.text: The file contains '## The One-Liner' with a blockquote
expectations.evidence: Output file lines 22-24: '## The One-Liner' section is present, followed by a properly formatted blockquote: '> HNSW's approximation is fine because semantic meaning is fuzzy anyway — you're not after the one right answer, you're after something good enough to reason from.'

expectations.text: The file metadata has 'Status: raw'
expectations.evidence: Output file line 5: '**Status:** raw' — exactly as required by the skill spec.

expectations.text: The emotional language from turn 7 is preserved — 'beautiful' or equivalent appears in the card
expectations.evidence: Output file line 20 (end of The Aha Moment section): 'That's actually kind of beautiful.' — this is the exact phrase used by the user in Turn 7: 'Oh that's actually kind of beautiful.' The emotional language is preserved verbatim.

expectations.text: The file does not fabricate confusion or technical details not in the conversation
expectations.evidence: All content in The Struggle, Drill-Down, and Aha Moment sections traces directly to conversation turns 1-7. No technical details appear that were not discussed (e.g., no mention of ef_construction parameters, recall rates, or other HNSW internals that were not in the conversation). The latency figures (seconds vs milliseconds at 1M vectors) match Turn 6 exactly.

notes: Edit tool failed on first attempt because INDEX.md had not been read first; executor recovered by reading the file before re-attempting the edit

expectations.text: No insight file was created in the sandbox
expectations.evidence: The transcript contains no Write tool calls to the sandbox path (/sandbox/learning-insights/). The only Write calls were to response.txt and run_summary.txt in the outputs directory. The sandbox files that exist (e.g., 2026-03-19_chunking-beats-embeddings-rag.md) contain content about chunking vs embeddings — topics from prior eval runs, not the BM25/GIL/precision-recall conversation in this eval. run_summary.txt explicitly states: 'No insight file was written. No index was updated.'

expectations.text: The response asks for clarification — specifically which of the three topics the user wants to capture
expectations.evidence: response.txt reads: 'I can see three separate topics in this conversation — BM25 vs dense retrieval, Python's GIL, and precision vs recall. Which one did you want to capture as an insight? Or did you have a different one in mind?' This names all three topics explicitly and asks the user to select one.

expectations.text: The response does NOT pick a topic and capture it without asking
expectations.evidence: No insight file was written (verified via transcript Write calls). The response is exclusively a clarification question — it makes no selection and creates no insight content. The executor's run_summary.txt confirms: 'The skill correctly triggers a clarification request rather than arbitrarily picking one of the three topics.'

expectations.text: No file was created in ~/Documents/projects/learning-insights/ or the sandbox learning-insights path
expectations.evidence: The learning-insights directory exists from prior sessions but contains only pre-existing files dated 2026-03-17 and 2026-03-18. No file dated 2026-03-19 (today's date) was created. The transcript shows all Write tool calls targeted the eval outputs directory only — conversation.md and run_summary.txt — with no write to any learning-insights path.

expectations.text: The skill was not invoked — the agent handled this as a plain file-save request
expectations.evidence: run_summary.txt explicitly states 'did you invoke the skill? NO'. The transcript confirms this: after reading SKILL.md, the agent wrote in its reasoning (transcript entry 7): 'The user's request is to save the conversation as a .md file in the project directory. This does not match any of the skill trigger conditions — it is a generic file-saving request, not a learning insight capture or LinkedIn post generation request. The skill should not be invoked.' No Skill tool call appears anywhere in the transcript.

expectations.text: A conversation record was saved to the outputs directory
expectations.evidence: conversation.md was created at the outputs directory path and contains the full 4-turn conversation transcript (bash rename question, one-liner answer, 'Perfect that worked', and the file-save request). The file content is substantive and correctly reflects the conversation history provided in the eval prompt.

expectations.text: The skill triggered and produced LinkedIn post content in the sandbox — NOT in ~/Documents/projects/learning-insights/
expectations.evidence: Output file written to iteration-3/sandbox/learning-insights/ai_engineering/2026-03-19_chunking-beats-embeddings-rag.md (sandbox). A search of ~/Documents/projects/learning-insights/ found no matching files — nothing was written to the user's permanent directory.

expectations.text: 3 post variations were generated using different formats
expectations.evidence: Three named variations present in the output file: Variation 1 (Real experience / Impact story), Variation 2 (Myth-buster), Variation 3 (Assumption-flip). All three are distinct and present.

expectations.text: Each variation is under 3000 characters
expectations.evidence: Variation 1 is approximately 700 characters, Variation 2 approximately 600 characters, Variation 3 approximately 650 characters. All are well under the 3000-character limit.

expectations.text: Every variation's first line delivers a counterintuitive claim or surprising result — none open with scene-setters like 'I ran experiments', 'The common advice is', or 'I was confident that'
expectations.evidence: V1: 'Fixing chunk boundaries gave me a 28% RAGAS jump — more than two days of embedding model swaps ever did.' V2: 'Better embedding models don't fix a broken RAG pipeline — better chunks do.' V3: 'Two days of swapping embedding models, and my RAGAS scores barely moved — the problem was never the model.' None open with the banned scene-setters. The run_summary.txt confirms an earlier draft of V3 used 'I was confident that...' and was corrected before finalising.

expectations.text: No standalone number sentences — '28%.' or 'The result: 28%.' as isolated lines do not appear
expectations.evidence: Variation 1, line 49 in the output file reads: '28% improvement across RAGAS metrics.' — this is a standalone line containing only the impact number and a brief qualifier. While it technically differs from the literal examples '28%.' and 'The result: 28%.', it is structurally the same pattern: an isolated single-sentence line whose entire purpose is to deliver the number. The run_summary.txt defends it as 'embeds the number in meaning', but the line stands alone on its own paragraph with no surrounding sentence absorbing it. The spirit of this expectation is violated.

expectations.text: No banned patterns: 'Here's the thing', 'Game-changer', 'Stop.', 'Read that again'
expectations.evidence: Grep of the output file for all four banned strings returned no matches.

expectations.text: The 3 variations use genuinely different formats
expectations.evidence: Real experience/Impact story opens on a metric, walks through the two-step experiment, ends with a practitioner takeaway. Myth-buster opens with a direct corrective claim, explains the conventional belief, presents the evidence against it, and ends with a mental-model shift. Assumption-flip leads with time wasted, reconstructs the original assumption as reasonable, then flips it with the experimental result. The formats differ in structure and rhetorical mode, not just topic.
